Elizabeth:
“My place is never in this world.
I sought and longed for something I could not quite name.

To be lost and to be found — that is the lifespan of love.

Better this way… to fade… with your eyes gazing upon me.”

comment_content:
My personal opinion about this film is generally positive 👍
It is not some epic masterpiece that one will remember for a lifetime and in my opinion  Oscar Isaac as Victor Frankenstein is not the best casting, but the film literally surprised me positively 👏
First of all: finally - NO gay and black propaganda !!!
For which applause again to the director 👏
It also lacks that crazy Hollywood feminism.
Frankenstein's creature in my opinion is shown quite well as a blank sheet of paper on which whatever you write, that is what you will read.
Real film music performed by a symphony orchestra instead of those typical sound experiments in most films like for example Tron Ares.
Was the film a little longer than it should be ?
For me personally – yes.
Maybe 15 minutes shorter would have been perfect in duration, but Del Toro likes long stories.
He still could have asked some Germans in Hollywood what the name ,,Frankenstein" really means = the stone of the Franks !
Also, it seems Guillermo is not aware, that if you can hold a stick of dynamite in your hand without the explosion tearing your body apart, then most likely most bullets will not be able to penetrate that same body.
But I stop with the criticism because my opinion is positive and overall I personally recommend this film specifically to all who are fans of the story.

reply_content: @marknewbold2583 No, as a European I happen to know that children over here, as well, often only come in contact with Frankenstein through pop-culture: Halloween masks, toys, characters based on Frankenstein's monster named "Frank" in tv-shows and video games, etc. So of course they come to believe that "Frankenstein" is the name of the monster, and the corporations that churn out the masks and toys and video games don't care about informing them otherwise. It also doesn't help that the book itself is often considered "too mature" for young children to read/have read to themselves, so the misunderstanding persists until the book ends up as school or course literature.

reply_content: ​​​@charlitaylor3623 Oh, I understand. When the Monster talks about his pain and loneliness, he's not lying; he really, really feels bad. When he says to himself: "Miserable, unhappy wretch!", "Was I, then, a monster, a blot upon the earth, from which all men fled and whom all men disowned? I cannot describe to you the agony that these reflections inflicted upon me; I tried to dispel them, but sorrow only increased with knowledge" - he's honest. The Monster's main problem is his perception of the world and himself in it. He didn't consider himself to be in any way connected, not just to people(even morally), but to all living beings and even to nature (I suppose reading Paradise Lost, where everything beautiful is created for people, didn't help the Monster accept himself in the world at all).  When he asked for a "Companion," he said, "My vices are the children of a forced solitude that I abhor, and my virtues will necessarily arise when I live in communion with an equal. I shall feel the affections of a sensitive being and become linked to the chain of existence and events from which I am now excluded" - which well illustrates the Monster's worldview. But he is also poor at reflecting on his actions and circumstances, even when he mentions his crimes in the final monologue - he does so in the context of the suffering his actions have caused him. And the Monster too often generalized ALL PEOPLE. He interpreted his good deeds as a great blessing, his crimes as forced circumstances, and protecting people (Felix protects his father, sister and wife) from the Monster (the threat he potentially poses) - as betrayal and rejection. The moment in the finale when the Мonster accuses all of humanity, but cites only two episodes;  and then explains to Walton, what Walton (and other people) supposedly think, that is, the Monster projects his thoughts onto others: “Still I desired love and fellowship, and I was still spurned. Was there no injustice in this? Am I to be thought the only criminal, when all humankind sinned against me? Why do you not hate Felix, who drove his friend from his door with contumely? Why do you not execrate the rustic who sought to destroy the savior of his child? Nay, these are virtuous and immaculate beings! I, the miserable and the abandoned, am an abortion, to be spurned at, and kicked, and trampled on. Even now my blood boils at the recollection of this injustice.” In fact, the Monster was most often rejected by the very people he imagined in his head.
